name: ginkgo biloba
mechanism: Proposed mechanism: Ginkgo extracts contain terpene lactones (e.g., ginkgolides) and other constituents; ginkgolide A is described as a platelet-activating factor (PAF) antagonist, which may relate to anticoagulant/bleeding concerns rather than a proven therapeutic pathway across indications.
activeCompounds: Ginkgolide A and bilobalide are two well-characterized ginkgo constituents commonly referenced in standardized extracts.
effects: No direct effects data. Contextual inference:
nan.. No direct mechanismofaction data. Contextual inference:
safetyNotes: NCCIH notes moderate-dose leaf extracts are likely safe for most adults and used safely up to 6 years in research, but seeds are toxic; common adverse effects include dizziness, GI symptoms, and headache. Bleeding risk and pregnancy cautions are emphasized. LiverTox states ginkgo has not been specifically linked to liver injury.

sources.title: Herb-Drug Interactions: What the Science Says (Ginkgo section)
The Ginkgo Evaluation of Memory (GEM) Study

description: A very ancient deciduous tree; in modern supplement use, leaf (usually extract) is most common form; seeds can be toxic if consumed.
dosage: 240 mg/day was the dose used in the GEM trial evaluating dementia prevention (ineffective in that context).
duration: No direct duration data. Contextual inference: ; nan; nan.. No direct mechanismofaction data. Contextual inference: ; nan; nan.. ; nan; nan. No direct effects data. interactions: NCCIH’s herb–drug interaction digest states that taking *Ginkgo biloba* with warfarin is associated with increased risk of major bleeding events; concomitant use with efavirenz is not recommended.
preparation: Typically leaf extract supplements; crude plant/seeds are not equivalent and seeds carry toxicity risk.
region: POWO lists native range as China (Zhejiang).
therapeuticUses: Used for memory loss, dementia, and circulatory issues
summary: Ginkgo leaf extract is widely marketed for cognitive and other claims, but NCCIH concludes there is no conclusive evidence of benefit for any condition, and the large GEM trial found 240 mg/day ineffective for reducing dementia/Alzheimer’s development in older adults. Safety cautions include bleeding risk (especially with warfarin), potential drug interactions (including efavirenz), and toxicity of seeds.
